title: Sudden Hind Leg Paralysis in Cats: Causes & Emergency Steps
meta_desc: If your cat's back legs are suddenly paralyzed, it is a critical emergency. Learn about Saddle Thrombus and the immediate steps you need to take.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind leg paralysis in cats is a life-threatening emergency typically caused by a blood clot; you must seek immediate veterinary care to manage pain and restore circulation.

What causes sudden hind leg paralysis in my cat?

Sudden loss of movement in the rear limbs of a cat is most often associated with Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), also known as a Saddle Thrombus. This occurs when a blood clot, typically originating from the heart, travels through the aorta and becomes lodged where the artery splits to supply the back legs. This blockage cuts off vital blood flow, leading to immediate paralysis and severe pain.

Is sudden hind leg paralysis in my cat a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a life-threatening medical emergency.
  • If your cat is dragging its back legs, vocalizing loudly in distress, or if the back paws feel cold to the touch, you must transport them to an emergency veterinary hospital immediately.
  • Minutes matter in these cases to manage pain and attempt to restore circulation.

How can a photo or video of my cat help the veterinary triage team?

  • A clear photo or short video of your cat’s posture and movement allows the veterinarian to identify "knuckling" of the paws or blue-tinted nail beds.
  • Identifying these classic signs of a blood clot helps the clinic prepare for your arrival in advance.
  • This visual information allows the triage team to prioritize your cat for immediate life-saving treatment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Sudden hind leg paralysis in cats can be caused by arterial thromboembolism, often presenting with posterior paresis or paralysis. The gastrocnemius muscles may be firm, and while the cat can often move the legs above the stifles, the tail is commonly unaffected, although sometimes only one hindlimb is affected. Diagnosis is based on clinical signs, physical examination, and Doppler blood flow readings of the hindlimbs, with ultrasound useful to identify the thromboembolus. Treatment includes analgesics, nursing care, and addressing any underlying disease such as cardiomyopathy. Anticoagulants like heparin may be used to reduce further thrombus formation. While neurologic deficits may improve over weeks to months, permanent deficits are possible, and the prognosis remains guarded.

Chapter: Cardiology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 127)

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they can't feel their legs?
faqs.answer: Yes, Saddle Thrombus is an extremely painful condition. Even if the legs are numb, the lack of blood flow and the pressure from the clot cause significant distress and require professional pain management.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the clot at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never administer human medications to cats. Aspirin can be toxic if not dosed specifically by a vet, and it will not resolve a major clot emergency. Seek professional care immediately.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of Saddle Thrombus?
faqs.answer: Common signs include sudden inability to use back legs, dragging the hindquarters, cold back paws, pale or blue paw pads, and loud vocalization due to pain.
